ppt.title: Chemical Reactions and Equations - Session 1
ppt.presentationGoal: To introduce the concept of chemical reactions, distinguish them from physical changes, and teach the basics of writing balanced chemical equations.

licensingNotes: Use Wikimedia Commons images by default and use in-app SVG/native slide text only as a fallback.
assetSearchPlan.fallbackStrategy: Use SVG diagrams with clear labels if specific Wikimedia assets are unavailable.

presentationWarnings: Do not confuse physical changes (melting ice) with chemical reactions.
Emphasize the Law of Conservation of Mass when balancing equations.

learningOutcomesCovered: Define a chemical reaction and identify signs
Write word and skeletal equations for simple reactions
omittedContent: Types of reactions in depth (Next Session)

slides.teachingBeat: Set the stage for the chapter, introduce the topic title, and engage students with a visual hook.
slides.presentonMarkdown:
# Chemical Reactions and Equations
> Chemical Reactions and Equations
> Session 1 of 5
- Chapter: Chemical Reactions and Equations

<!-- layout: concept-split -->
<!-- purpose: title-slide -->

<!-- speaker-notes
Purpose: Set the stage for the chapter, introduce the topic title, and engage students with a visual hook.
Purpose: Introduce the chapter title and set expectations.
Explain: Today we begin our journey into how matter transforms. We will learn that nothing is truly 'lost' in a reaction, just rearranged.
Ask: Have you ever seen rust form on an old bike? Or watched bread rise? These are chemical reactions happening around us daily.
Transition: Let's look at what actually happens when substances change chemically.
Explain: Chapter: Chemical Reactions and Equations
Explain: Student takeaway: Understanding that matter changes form through chemical reactions.
-->

- 3. Write Word Equations

slides.teachingBeat: Define the core concept and contrast it with physical changes to prevent misconceptions.
mustTeach: Not just a shape or state change
slides.presentonMarkdown:
# Chemical Reactions Defined
> Chemical Reaction:
> - New Substance Formed
- - Properties Change
- - Irreversible (usually)
- Properties change (color, smell)

<!-- layout: concept-split -->
<!-- purpose: concept-intro -->

<!-- speaker-notes
Purpose: Define the core concept and contrast it with physical changes to prevent misconceptions.
Purpose: Define the term 'chemical reaction' clearly.
Explain: A chemical reaction is a process where substances change into entirely new substances. Unlike melting ice, you can't easily turn rust back into iron just by cooling it down.
Ask: If I melt an ice cube, did I make water? Yes. Did I make something NEW that wasn't there before? No, it was already H2O frozen.
Correct: Students often think melting is a chemical reaction. Clarify that state changes are physical; new substance formation is chemical.
Explain: Not just a shape or state change
Explain: - Properties Change
-->
slides.teacherIntent: Define the core concept and contrast it with physical changes to prevent misconceptions.
slides.studentTakeaway: A chemical reaction creates new substances with different properties.
speakerNotes: Purpose: Define the core concept and contrast it with physical changes to prevent misconceptions.
Purpose: Define the term 'chemical reaction' clearly.
Explain: A chemical reaction is a process where substances change into entirely new substances. Unlike melting ice, you can't easily turn rust back into iron just by cooling it down.
Ask: If I melt an ice cube, did I make water? Yes. Did I make something NEW that wasn't there before? No, it was already H2O frozen.
Correct: Students often think melting is a chemical reaction. Clarify that state changes are physical; new substance formation is chemical.
Explain: Not just a shape or state change
Explain: - Properties Change
slides.visualPlan: A comparison diagram showing 'Physical Change' (Ice cube -> Water drop) vs 'Chemical Reaction' (Iron nail -> Rust flake). Use arrows to show the transformation.

assets.attributionText: Tian B, Poulter C, Jacobson M

animationHints: Show physical change first, then reveal chemical reaction to contrast.

slides.teachingBeat: List the observable evidence that a chemical reaction has occurred.
slides.presentonMarkdown:
# How Do We Know It Happened?
> - Change in Color
> - Evolution of Gas
- - Temperature Change
- - Formation of Precipitate
- Temperature Change (Heat/Light)

<!-- layout: concept-split -->
<!-- purpose: evidence-slide -->

<!-- speaker-notes
Purpose: List the observable evidence that a chemical reaction has occurred.
Purpose: Provide concrete evidence students can look for.
Explain: We use our senses to detect reactions. If a liquid turns cloudy, bubbles appear without boiling, or the container gets hot/cold, something chemical is happening.
Ask: What happens when you mix vinegar and baking soda? Do we see anything?
Listen for: 'Bubbles!' Confirm this is gas evolution (CO2).
Board work: Draw a simple beaker with bubbles rising to illustrate gas evolution.
Explain: - Temperature Change
-->
slides.slideTitle: How Do We Know It Happened?
slides.teacherIntent: List the observable evidence that a chemical reaction has occurred.
slides.studentTakeaway: Color change, gas production, temperature change, and precipitate formation are key signs.
speakerNotes: Purpose: List the observable evidence that a chemical reaction has occurred.
Purpose: Provide concrete evidence students can look for.
Explain: We use our senses to detect reactions. If a liquid turns cloudy, bubbles appear without boiling, or the container gets hot/cold, something chemical is happening.
Ask: What happens when you mix vinegar and baking soda? Do we see anything?
Listen for: 'Bubbles!' Confirm this is gas evolution (CO2).
Board work: Draw a simple beaker with bubbles rising to illustrate gas evolution.
Explain: - Temperature Change
slides.visualPlan: A 4-quadrant grid showing each sign. Top-left: Color change (e.g., litmus paper). Top-right: Gas bubbles in a test tube. Bottom-left: Thermometer rising/falling. Bottom-right: Cloudy precipitate forming.

assets.altText: pH test strips, also known as pH indicator strips or pH litmus strips, are simple and convenient tools used to measure the pH of a solution. pH is a measure of the acidity or alkalinity of a solution and is represented on a scale ranging from 0 to 14. A pH of 7 is considered neutral, values below 7 are acidic, and values above 7 are alkaline (basic).

animationHints: Reveal each quadrant one by one as the teacher discusses it.

slides.teachingBeat: Demonstrate how to translate a chemical observation into a word equation.
slides.presentonMarkdown:
# Example: Magnesium Burning
> Reaction: Burning of Magnesium Ribbon
> Word Equation:
- Magnesium + Oxygen → Magnesium Oxide
- (Reactants) (Products)
![Example: Magnesium Burning](http://localhost:3002/api/ppt-assets/823d4786671b31ca87bcc3d4257b42b9ed62bd1e.svg)

<!-- layout: concept-split -->
<!-- purpose: worked-example-1 -->

<!-- speaker-notes
Purpose: Demonstrate how to translate a chemical observation into a word equation.
Purpose: Show the structure of a word equation.
Explain: We start with what we put in (reactants), add an arrow to show change, and end with what comes out (products).
Ask: In this reaction, which is the reactant? Which is the product?
Listen for: 'Magnesium' as reactant. Correct if they say 'Oxygen' only.
Board work: Write 'Reactants -> Products' above the equation to reinforce structure.
Explain: Magnesium + Oxygen → Magnesium Oxide
-->
slides.teacherIntent: Demonstrate how to translate a chemical observation into a word equation.
slides.studentTakeaway: Reactants go on the left, products on the right, separated by an arrow.
speakerNotes: Purpose: Demonstrate how to translate a chemical observation into a word equation.
Purpose: Show the structure of a word equation.
Explain: We start with what we put in (reactants), add an arrow to show change, and end with what comes out (products).
Ask: In this reaction, which is the reactant? Which is the product?
Listen for: 'Magnesium' as reactant. Correct if they say 'Oxygen' only.
Board work: Write 'Reactants -> Products' above the equation to reinforce structure.
Explain: Magnesium + Oxygen → Magnesium Oxide

slides.teachingBeat: Engage students in identifying components of a new reaction.
slides.presentonMarkdown:
# Practice: Identifying Reactants & Products
> Reaction: Heating of Iron and Sulfur Powder
- Question:
- - What are the reactants?
- - What is the product formed?
- Reactants: Iron (Fe) + Sulfur (S)

<!-- layout: text-light -->
<!-- purpose: guided-practice-1 -->

<!-- speaker-notes
Purpose: Engage students in identifying components of a new reaction.
Purpose: Guided practice to reinforce learning.
Explain: We mix iron filings with sulfur powder and heat them. They combine to form a black solid called Iron Sulphide.
Ask: Can anyone tell me what we start with? What do we end up with?
Listen for: 'Iron' and 'Sulfur'. Correct if they say 'Heat' is a reactant (it's energy, not matter).
Transition: Now that we know the words, how do we write this using symbols?
Explain: Product: Iron Sulphide (FeS)
-->
slides.templateSlideTitle: Let's Try Together: Iron and Sulfur
slides.teacherIntent: Engage students in identifying components of a new reaction.
slides.studentTakeaway: I can identify reactants and products for the Iron + Sulfur reaction.
onSlideText: Reaction: Heating of Iron and Sulfur Powder
speakerNotes: Purpose: Engage students in identifying components of a new reaction.
Purpose: Guided practice to reinforce learning.
Explain: We mix iron filings with sulfur powder and heat them. They combine to form a black solid called Iron Sulphide.
Ask: Can anyone tell me what we start with? What do we end up with?
Listen for: 'Iron' and 'Sulfur'. Correct if they say 'Heat' is a reactant (it's energy, not matter).
Transition: Now that we know the words, how do we write this using symbols?
Explain: Product: Iron Sulphide (FeS)
slides.wikimediaSearchLabel: iron filings and sulfur powder mixture
slides.visualPlan: A split image showing a pile of grey iron filings and yellow sulfur powder on the left (reactants), and a black lump of Iron Sulphide on the right (product).

slides.teachingBeat: Summarize key takeaways and bridge to the next session on balancing equations.
mustTeach: - New substances form in chemical changes
slides.presentonMarkdown:
# Recap: Chemical Reactions & Equations
> Key Takeaways:
> 1. Chemical Reactions create new matter.
- 2. Look for color change, gas, heat, or precipitates.
- 3. Word equations: Reactants + Reactant → Product.
- - New substances form in chemical changes

<!-- layout: concept-split -->
<!-- purpose: summary-bridge -->

<!-- speaker-notes
Purpose: Summarize key takeaways and bridge to the next session on balancing equations.
Purpose: Consolidate learning and set up the next lesson.
Explain: We've learned how to spot a reaction and write it in words. But chemists use symbols (like Fe, O2) for precision.
Ask: Why do we need symbol equations instead of just word ones?
Listen for: 'More specific' or 'Shorter'.
Transition: Next time, we will learn how to write these using chemical formulas and ensure they are balanced according to the Law of Conservation of Mass.
Explain: - Signs: Color, Gas, Heat, Precipitate
-->
slides.teacherIntent: Summarize key takeaways and bridge to the next session on balancing equations.
slides.studentTakeaway: We can identify reactions and write word equations; now we need balanced symbol equations.
bulletPoints: 2. Look for color change, gas, heat, or precipitates.
- New substances form in chemical changes

# Your Task for Tonight
> Homework:
> Write the word equation for these reactions:
- 1. Burning of Magnesium
- 2. Reaction between Iron and Sulfur
- 3. Decomposition of Calcium Carbonate (Heating)

keyInformation: Definition of Chemical Reaction: Formation of new substances.
Signs of Change: Color change, gas evolution, temperature change, precipitate formation.
Word Equation Structure: Reactants + Reactant -> Product.
